talk_title: Integrating multi-omics and novel modalities to map EGFR TKI resistance in NSCLC

speaker.name: Daniel S.W. Tan
speaker.affiliation: University of California, San Francisco, Department of Oncology

targets.dependency_context: TKI‑treated NSCLC with high CD47 expression

biomarkers.name: EGFR ctDNA persistence
biomarkers.finding: Patients with persistent EGFR ctDNA after third‑generation TKI had shorter PFS (median 5.2 mo vs 9.8 mo) in retrospective cohort

biomarkers.name: cMET amplification
biomarkers.finding: Detected in 15‑20% of resistant biopsies; co‑targeting yielded partial responses in 4/9 evaluable patients

biomarkers.name: P53 co‑mutation
biomarkers.finding: Associated with median PFS 4.1 mo vs 7.6 mo in EGFR‑mutant cohort (HR 0.62, p=0.03)

biomarkers.name: Stromal TME signature (activated neutrophils, CAFs)
biomarkers.finding: High signature score correlated with 2‑year relapse rate 48% vs 22% (p=0.004)

models.name: NSCLC EGFR‑TKI resistance cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: EGFR mutant, pre‑ and post‑treatment biopsies, n=36

models.name: Persistent EGFR ctDNA + chemotherapy trial (North America)
models.genotype_or_key_features: EGFR mutant NSCLC with ctDNA persistence, randomized 1:1, n=112

clinical_data.metric: Patients with no known resistance mechanism

MOA_summary: Tan described how sequential EGFR TKIs select for on‑target (e.g., C797S) and off‑target alterations (MET amplification, lineage plasticity) and how multi‑omics (DNA, RNA, spatial transcriptomics, proteomics) can map these states. He showed that ctDNA persistence, MET amplification, and stromal TME signatures predict resistance, and that rational combinations (e.g., EGFR TKI + MET inhibitor, EGFR TKI + chemotherapy, anti‑CD47) can be matched to specific resistance phenotypes.
key_findings: EGFR TKIs achieve high initial ORR (60‑80%) but 50‑60% of patients on frontline third‑gen inhibitors lack any detectable resistance mechanism by NGS.
On‑target resistance mutations (e.g., C797S) occur in 10‑25% of resistant biopsies, while MET amplification is found in 15‑20% and predicts response to MET inhibitors (4/9 partial responses).
Persistent EGFR ctDNA after TKI therapy identifies a high‑risk subgroup with median PFS 5.2 mo vs 9.8 mo (p<0.01).
Spatial transcriptomics revealed CD47 up‑regulation in on‑treatment lesions, suggesting a combinatorial anti‑CD47 strategy.
Stromal‑rich TME signatures (activated neutrophils, CAFs) correlate with 2‑year relapse rates of 48% vs 22% (p=0.004), defining a poor‑risk population for upfront combination trials.
Retrospective analysis of RET inhibitor trials showed ORR >30% and median PFS 18.4 mo, yet phase III trials were terminated due to enrollment challenges.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Fourth‑generation EGFR TKIs produce deep ctDNA reductions but limited radiographic responses, indicating that target inhibition alone may not overcome lineage plasticity.
Lineage transition to neuroendocrine‑like phenotype observed in resistant single‑cell profiles, suggesting a non‑genetic resistance route.

combination_strategies.rationale: Add cytotoxic pressure to eradicate clones with persistent EGFR ctDNA after TKI
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved PFS in ctDNA‑positive subgroup (median 7.4 mo vs 5.2 mo)

combination_strategies.rationale: Target MET amplification as a bypass driver in EGFR‑TKI resistant disease
combination_strategies.phenotype: Partial responses in 44% of MET‑amplified patients

combination_strategies.rationale: Combine with osimertinib to suppress concurrent RET rearrangements
combination_strategies.phenotype: Objective responses observed in case series (n=3)

combination_strategies.rationale: Counteract CD47 up‑regulation identified by spatial transcriptomics in on‑treatment lesions
combination_strategies.phenotype: Preclinical synergy with EGFR TKI (tumor regression p<0.01)

open_questions: What is the optimal molecular threshold for defining MET amplification as a therapeutic target?
Can ctDNA persistence be validated as a surrogate endpoint for early efficacy in phase II trials?
How can real‑world molecular databases be leveraged to generate robust external controls for rare‑driver trials?
